does: This paper presents Safe Crowd Preference-based RL, a hierarchical framework that extracts shared safety criteria from crowd preference datasets and transfers them to downstream tasks. The core contribution is identifying that different users in crowd preference data often express distinct task preferences but converge on similar underlying safety principles. The authors first demonstrate that naive reward combination—jointly optimizing crowd-learned and task-specific rewards—fails to properly enforce safety constraints. They propose instead a two-level approach that learns safety-aligned skills from crowd preferences as a low-level policy component, then trains a high-level policy to compose these skills while solving downstream tasks. Experimental validation spans multiple safe RL benchmarks and an LLM-style setting, showing the method substantially reduces safety violations compared to baselines without requiring explicit safety annotations.
connects: This work builds on the established pipeline of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), which has become central to aligning language models and agents with human values. It extends prior research on learning from preference comparisons by recognizing that preference data contains latent structure beyond task completion—specifically, implicit consensus around safety principles even when explicit task objectives diverge. The paper responds to limitations in existing multi-objective RL and reward combination methods, which typically treat all learned objectives symmetrically. It connects to hierarchical RL approaches that decompose policies into complementary levels, adapting these ideas to the safety transfer problem. The work also engages with broader literature on implicit alignment and value learning, where safety emerges as an unintended consequence of preference-based training rather than explicit constraint specification.
enables: This approach opens pathways for more robust safety transfer in RL systems by leveraging naturally-occurring crowd consensus around safety without expensive explicit annotation. It points toward mechanisms for discovering shared values and constraints across heterogeneous human preferences, applicable beyond safety to other implicit objectives (fairness, interpretability, robustness). The hierarchical decomposition framework could be extended to multi-level skill hierarchies that extract multiple implicit objectives and compose them flexibly. Future work might scale this to larger, more diverse crowd datasets and explore how implicit safety learned from one domain transfers to out-of-distribution tasks. This also suggests potential improvements to RLHF pipelines for language models by mining safety principles from existing preference data without requiring new annotations, and raises questions about automatically discovering what implicit objectives different user groups prioritize.
